question: What kind of protocol is HTTP at its core?
options: A binary streaming protocol with no defined message boundaries
A text-based request/response protocol running over TCP
A peer-to-peer protocol with no client or server role
An encryption protocol that replaces TLS
explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is a text-based request/response protocol: a client sends one request, the server sends one response, and the header portion is human-readable ASCII. It rides on top of TCP, which provides the reliable byte stream underneath.

question: An HTTP message is made of four parts in order. Which is correct?
options: Body, headers, blank line, start line
Start line, blank line, headers, body
Start line, headers, blank line, optional body
Headers, start line, body, checksum
explanation: Every HTTP message is a start line (request line or status line), then header lines, then a blank line (CRLF by itself) marking the end of headers, then an optional body. The blank line is how the receiver knows the headers are finished.

question: A client sends a request that times out with no reply. For which method is it safe to simply resend the same request?
options: POST, because it creates a new resource each time
PUT, because it is idempotent — sending it again has the same effect as once
Any method, since HTTP retries are always safe
No method can ever be safely retried
explanation: Idempotent methods (GET, PUT, DELETE) produce the same result whether sent once or many times, so a client may safely retry them after a timeout. POST is not idempotent — resending it might create a second resource or charge a card twice.

question: A response comes back with status code 403. What does its family tell you, and what does the specific code mean?
options: 5xx server error; the server crashed
4xx client error; the caller is authenticated but not permitted to do this
3xx redirection; the resource moved
4xx client error; the caller has not authenticated yet
explanation: The leading 4 marks a client error — the request was wrong, so fix the caller. 403 Forbidden specifically means the client IS authenticated but is not allowed to access the resource. That contrasts with 401 Unauthorized, which means not authenticated at all.

question: On a keep-alive connection carrying several responses back to back, how does the client know where one response's body ends and the next response begins?
options: The TCP connection closes after each body
It reads exactly Content-Length bytes (or the chunked terminator) for the body
A special magic byte separates every response
It guesses based on how long recv() blocks
explanation: TCP is a byte stream with no message boundaries, so HTTP must reintroduce them. The receiver reads headers up to the blank line, then reads exactly Content-Length more bytes as the body. Without a close to mark the end, honoring that length is what keeps consecutive responses from bleeding together.

question: Why does reusing one HTTP/1.1 connection for many requests (keep-alive) make a service noticeably faster under load?
options: It compresses the request bodies automatically
It skips the TCP three-way handshake on every request after the first
It switches the requests from TCP to UDP
It lets the server skip sending status codes
explanation: Opening a TCP connection costs a three-way handshake — a full round-trip — before any HTTP bytes move. HTTP/1.1 keeps the connection open by default, so subsequent requests reuse it and pay that handshake cost only once. That is why connection pooling (a shared Session) is a major client-side performance win.

question: An API endpoint replies 405 Method Not Allowed to a DELETE request. What is the most accurate reading?
options: The server crashed while handling the DELETE
The route (target) exists, but it does not accept the DELETE method
The client is not authenticated
The resource was permanently moved elsewhere
explanation: 405 is a 4xx client error meaning the target resource exists but the method used is not supported on it — for example a read-only endpoint that allows GET but not DELETE. A well-behaved server also returns an Allow header listing the methods it does accept. It is not a 5xx (server fault) or an auth error.
